revision.text:
[[ja:到津八幡神社]] {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Itozu Hachiman Shrine | image = File:Itouzu shrine.jpg | location = 1-8-1 Kamiitozu, Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyushu-shi, Fukuoka-ken, Japan | coordinates= {{Coord|33.877808333333334|N|130.8532972222222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|qid=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}}
{{ill|Tagorihime|lt=Tagorihime|lt=Takiribime|ja|多紀理比賣命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiribime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比賣命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
{{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸嶋比賣命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
{{ill|Toyohiwake no Mikoto|ja|豊日別命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyohiwake no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 豊日別}} | status = Former Prefectural Shrine | founded = | festival = | Rituals = | prefecture= {{ill|Kokurakita-ku|qid=Q1042291|1=Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū}} }} '''Itozu Hachiman Shrine''' (いとうづはちまんじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|en|Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|qid=Q1042291|lt=Kokurakita-ku|1=Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|en|Kitakyushu|qid=Q188806|lt=Kitakyushu|1=Kitakyushu}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|qid=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}, Japan. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Ken-sha|lt=Prefectural Shrine|lt2=Prefectural shrine (Japan)|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ken-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|qid=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}} * {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}} * {{ill|Munakata Three Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Three Goddesses|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}} ({{ill|Tagorihime|lt=Tagorihime|lt=Takiribime|ja|多紀理比賣命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiribime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比賣命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸嶋比賣命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) * {{ill|Toyohiwake no Mikoto|ja|豊日別命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyohiwake no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 豊日別}} == History == The name "Itozu" is derived from an event where {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}}’s boat arrived at this location during her return from the {{ill|Three Han Campaign|ja|三韓征伐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Han Campaign|qq=}} campaign. A shrine was built here to honor her peaceful spirit (和魂), and it became a site of prayer for safe childbirth. The nearby river, used as a bathing site for newborns, came to be known as "Ubuyu River" (産川). In 1184 (Bunji 4), the shrine was merged with {{ill|Usa Jingū|en|Usa Jingū|qid=Q715632|lt=Usa Jingū|1=Usa Jingū}} under the order of Emperor {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|en|Emperor Go-Toba|qid=Q316658|lt=Go-Toba|1=Emperor Go-Toba}}, and it came to be called Itozu Hachiman Shrine. During the late 16th century, the shrine suffered significant damage, including the loss of sacred treasures, but it was later restored in 1600 (Keichō 5) by {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|en|Hosokawa Tadaoki|qid=Q1147154|lt=Hosokawa Tadaoki|1=Hosokawa Tadaoki}} after his relocation to Kokura. In 1873 (Meiji 6), the shrine was designated as a village shrine (郷社) and was promoted to prefectural shrine (県社) in 1925 (Taishō 14). == Features == Itozu Hachiman Shrine is widely visited by individuals praying for safe childbirth and children's health due to its historical association with {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}}. Additionally, a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} named "Nishiharu Inari Shrine," once located on a site in Tokyo, was relocated to this shrine along with a stone bridge originally from the same location. == Access == * The shrine is accessible via a 4-minute walk from Shimoitozu Bus Stop on the Nishitetsu Bus network. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Citation |author=Dai Nippon Jingikai Fukuoka Branch |title=福岡県神社誌 下巻 (Fukuoka Prefecture Shrine History, Vol. 2) |publisher=Dai Nippon Jingikai |year=1945 |url={{NDLDC|1040147}} }} * {{Citation |author=Itozu Community Development Council |title=子どもたちに伝えたい到津の郷土史 わがまち知っちょる編 (Itozu Local History for Children) |publisher=Itozu Community Development Council |year=2018 }} [[ja:到津八幡神社]] {{translated page|ja|到津八幡神社|version=95139869|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{wikidata link|Q104533567}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:History of Kokura-Kita Ward]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
